title: Abstract
content: CdS nanoparticles were precipitated by the reaction of cadmium acetate with sodium sulphide in the presence of cetyltrimethylammonium (CTA) and deposited on montmorillonite (MMT).The resulting CdS-MMT nanocomposite contained 6 wt. of CdS and 30 wt. of CTA.Band-gap energy of CdS was estimated at 2.63 0.09 eV using the Tauc plot.The size of CdS nanoparticles was calculated from the bandgap energy at 5 nm and from the micrographs of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) at 5 nm.Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) recognized the cubic structure of CdS (Hawleite).The dynamic light scattering (DLS) method confirmed that CdS nanoparticles were anchored on the surface of MMT particles.CTA was found to be intercalated into MMT and adsorbed on its external surface.CdS-MMT was used for the photoreduction of carbon dioxide dissolved in NaOH solutions.The yields of originating gas products can be arranged in the order: H 2 ) CH 4 CO.Amounts of these products were 4-8 folds higher then those obtained with TiO 2 Evonic P25.Hydrogen reduced CO 2 to CO and CH 4 .

title: Experimental
content: The used chemicals were of analytical reagent grade: cadmium acetate, sodium sulphide, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (all from Lachema, Czech Republic).Water deionized by reverse osmosis (Aqua Osmotic, Czech Republic) was used for the preparation of all solutions.Na -rich montmorillonite SWy 2 (Crook County, Wyoming) with CEC of 1.21 0.06 meq g A1 determined by the saturation with NH 4 was used as a carrier for CdS nanoparticles.The structural MMT formula (Na 0.40 K 0.02 Ca 0.01 )(Al 1.31 Mg 0.39 Fe 3 0:25 Ti 0.02 ) (Si 4 )O 10 (OH) 2 was calculated from the results of X-ray fluorescence analysis of MMT.The 5 lm fraction separated by sedimentation was used for all experiments.The TiO 2 photocatalyst Evonic P25 was purchased from Precheza (Czech Republic).TiO 2 properties are given in Table 1 34. CdS nanoparticles were prepared according to the method already used for the preparation of ZnS nanoparticles in our earlier work 35: CdS nanoparticles were precipitated by the reaction of cadmium acetate and sodium sulphide in the presence of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide.The originated CdS nanodispersion was shaken with montmorillonite for 24 h.The resulting nanocomposite of CdS and MMT (CdS-MMT) was then filtered and dried at 60-70 C. The content of cadmium in CdS-MMT was determined after its dissolution in a mixture of HClO 4 HNO 3 HF 36 and calculated from amounts in solutions before and after shaking with MMT.Cadmium determinations were performed using an atomic adsorption spectrometer AA280FS (Varian Inc., Austria) with the acetylene-air atomization. UV-VIS spectra of CdS dispersions (without montmorillonite) were measured in 1 cm quartz cuvettes by an UV-Vis spectrometer Lambda 35 (Perkin Elmer, USA). X-ray powder diffraction was performed by a powder diffractometer (INEL, France) equipped with a curved position-sensitive detector PSD 120 MB11 (reflection mode, Ge-monochromatized, Cu Ka1 radiation).XRD patterns were recorded in an ambient atmosphere under constant conditions (1500 s, 60 kV, 55 mA). Transmission electron microscopy of CdS nanoparticles was performed with a JEM 2010 (Jeol, Japan) electron microscope at 160 kV of the acceleration voltage.For the TEM measurements, a drop of the aqueous dispersion of CdS-MMT was deposited on copper grids with carbon layer and dried at room temperatures.Using TEM, the selected area electron diffraction analysis was carried out as well. 2.3.4.1.Scanning electron microscopy.An XL 30 Philips SEM instrument (Netherlands) equipped with a Robinson backscattered electron (BSE) detector was used for examination of the CdS morphology and cadmium and sulphur distribution on the montmorillonite surface.The powdered samples were coated with gold and palladium in an ionization chamber before examination. Dynamic light scattering was measured at k 633 nm using a Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (Malvern Instruments).The powdered samples were dispersed in deionized water to obtain the 0.1 wt. dispersions that were consequently forced by cavitation dynamics in a flow with the velocity gradient of 26 m s A1 mm during 4.5 min.The dispersions were further forced by ultrasonic cavitation in a field with the density power of 100 W l A1 during 10 min.The filtration fraction 450 nm (membrane filter) was used for the DLS measurements. The photoreduction of carbon dioxide was carried out in a home-made apparatus 35.A stirred batch annular reactor with a suspended catalyst illuminated by the UV 8 W Hg-lamp (254 nm) was used.Gas reaction products were analysed by gas chromatography (see bellow).The CO 2 diffusion from the gas phase through the gas-liquid interface in the reactor was eliminated by saturating aqueous NaOH solutions of 0.2 mmol l A1 with pure CO 2 before the start of experiments.During the saturation, pH of CO 2 solutions decreased from 12.2 to 6.7. The CdS-MMT loading of 1 g l A1 was chosen to avoid concentration gradients in a bulk of stirred CdS-MMT dispersions due to a light scattering effect as a result of the high CdS-MMT content.A suitable volume of 100 ml of the liquid phase was used to ensure perfect mixing in this annular photoreactor 37. The gas products were analyzed using a gas chromatograph (GC-Agilent Technologies 6890 N) equipped with FID and TCD detectors (GCFIDTCD) and the Molsieve and HP Poraplot Q columns for the analysis of methane, hydrogen, ethane, carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide and oxygen.The calibration with certified calibration gases (1.5 mol CH 4 , 0.987 mol H 2 , 0.0493 mol C 2 H 6 , 2.02 mol CO, and 99.999 mol CO 2 ) was performed before each experimental run. The experimental data were statistically evaluated using the QC.Expert programme (TriloByte, Pardubice, Czech Republic) at a 0.05 significance level.

title: Results and discussion
content: CdS nanoparticles were precipitated in the presence of CTA to prevent the CdS agglomeration.The CTA concentration in final dispersions was about 4 mmol l A1 that is above its critical micellar concentration (1 mmol l A1 ).Therefore, CTA was assumed to create micelles, in which CdS nanoparticles were encapsulated.CdSCTA micelles were likely agglomerated and adsorbed on the MMT surface 38.The total amount of cadmium in CdS-MMT was determined at 0.6 mmol g A1 , i.e. 6 wt. of CdS in CdS-MMT. CdS nanoparticles together with CTA created yellow transparent dispersions.A typical VIS absorption spectrum is shown in Fig. 1A.It is well known that the onset of absorption (absorption edge) depends on nanoparticle size.As a result of the quantum size effect, the absorption edge shifts to lower wavelengths with the decrease of particle size.Compared to bulk CdS having the absorption edge at 600 nm 39, it is obvious that the absorption onset was blueshifted to 517 nm.This phenomenon is generally considered to be an evidence of the presence of nanoparticles. The obtained VIS spectra of CdS nanoparticles were used for the evaluation of band-gap energy (E bg ) using the Tauc equation 40: where e is the absorption coefficient, hm is the energy of incident photons and r is the power, which characterizes the electronic transition during absorption processes in the K-space.Specially, r is 12, 32, 2 and 3 for direct allowed, direct forbidden, indirect allowed and indirect forbidden transitions, respectively.The parameter C depends on the transition probability that can be considered to be constant within the optical frequency range. The usual method for the determination of E bg involves plotting (ehv) 1r against (hv).The Tauc plot with r 12 for the direct bandgap corresponding to the visual CdS spectrum (Fig. 1A) is shown in Fig. 1B.Instead of the absorption coefficient e, absorbance A defined by the Lambert-Beer law (A e l c, where l and c are the optical path length and concentration of absorbing compounds, respectively) was used.The CdS band-gap energy was calculated at 2.63 0.09 eV (n 4).In comparison with E bg 2.42 eV of bulk CdS it proves the formation of CdS nanoparticles, which were indicated by the blue-shifted VIS spectra. The obtained band-gap energy was used for the estimation of CdS nanoparticles size in terms of the well-known Brus equation 41.The nanoparticle radius R can be estimated as follows: where E bulk and E nano are the band bap energies of bulk and nanosized semiconductors, respectively, h is the Planck s constant, m e and m h are the effective masses of electron and hole, respectively, and e is the dielectric constant of the material.Here, m e 0.19m 0 and m h 0.8m 0 , where m 0 is the free electron mass and the dielectric constant e 5.78 42.The calculated CdS nanoparticle radius corresponding to E bg 2.63 eV was R 2.5 nm. In order to observe the location of CdS nanoparticles on MMT the XRD patterns of CdS-MMT were obtained.It was found that the interlayer distance d(0 0 1) of MMT increased from 1.20 nm to 1.90 nm, which can be explained by the intercalation of CTA forming monolayeres 43.The intensity of the 0 0 1 diffraction peak increased likely due to the stabilization of the tabular MMT structure.The inner (interlayer) surface of montmorillonite is about an order of magnitude larger than the external surface and, therefore, most of CTA should be loaded between MMT layers. As the interlayer distance increased only by about 0.70 nm, it is very likely that CdS nanoparticles were located on the external MMT surface.All significant diffraction peaks shown in Fig. 2 were attributed to montmorillonite but no significant CdS diffractions were observed.It indicates that deposited CdS nanoparticles were of very small size. The scanning electron micrographs demonstrate the morphology of montmorillonite as well as the location of CdS nanoparticles, e.g.see Fig. 3.The SEM device equipped with a qualitative X-ray facility was able to map the placement of cadmium and sulphur on the CdS-MMT surface.From the micrographs with the scale of 50 lm, it is obvious that the nanoparticles were equally distributed over the examined MMT surface. The size of CdS nanoparticles was estimated using the TEM micrographs.TEM micrographs of CdS-MMT with the scales of 100 nm and 200 nm are demonstrated in Fig. 4. Fig. 4a shows many small spherical nanoparticles agglomerated in larger ones by means of CTA.These agglomerates were formed by the breakdown of CdSCTA micelles.The nanoparticle diameters (D) were measured manually; their normal distribution was confirmed by statistical tests.The mean diameter was evaluated at 5.0 0.25 nm (n 48).It means that the deposited nanoparticles had the similar sizes as those in the aqueous dispersion (2R 5.0 nm) calculated by Eq. ( 2). In addition, in the micrographs with lower resolutions (Fig. 4B), there are small aggregates of CdS and CTA located on the MMT surface and some larger ones are placed alongside MMT as a result of the sample preparation for the TEM analysis.The layered MMT structure is apparent in Fig. 4B.The content of CTA in CdS-MMT found by gravimetry was about 30 wt.. Selected area electron diffraction was performed to indentify the crystallite structure of CdS.The SAED patterns (an inset in Fig. 4A) revealed the existence of cubic CdS known as Hawleite. The used DSL technique is based on the measurements of time dependent light intensity fluctuation caused by the Brown movement of particles in dispersion.The interval of recognizable particle sizes is from 0.6 nm to 6000 nm.In diluted dispersions, the densities of particles are very low and thus mean distances among them are much higher then their size.Therefore, very diluted CdS-MMT dispersions (0.1 wt.) were prepared.Its submicron fractions was filtered off through 400 nm mesh filters and, consequently, the DSL measurements were performed to observe particles sizes in the filtrates.The diluted dispersions of MMT were processed and measured in the same manner for comparison. The volume distributions of particles in CdS-MMT and MMT submicron fractions are demonstrated in Fig. 5.Both distributions correspond to the lognormal distributions with modus sizes of 208 nm for MMT particles and 237 nm for CdS-MMT ones.The particle size in MMT fractions ranged from 80 nm to 450 nm.The smallest particles correspond to individual MMT crystallinites; the larger ones were created by their agglomeration.In case of CdS-MMT, the particles of 100 nm to 500 nm were found.The large particles were likely created by the agglomeration of MMT crystallinites with CTA. Primarily we assumed that CdS nanoparticles will be separated from MMT agglomerates into the aqueous phase.In order to observe these nanoparticles, the CdS-MMT dispersions were also filtered through the filters with mesh size of 100 nm and the filtrates were analysed by DLS.CdS nanoparticles with the modus size about 30 nm were observed as demonstrated by an inset in Fig. 5.In spite of vigorous disintegration by cavitation dynamics and ultrasonic cavitation, their content in CdS-MMT submicron fractions was about 1.4, which was estimated by weighting of the evaporates.It indicates that majority of CdS nanoparticles were attached to the MMT surface andor incorporated in MMTCTA agglomerates.Taking into account CdS nanoparticles size about 5 nm discussed in the previous chapters, the 30 nm particles were likely formed by aggregation of the smaller ones. CdS-MMT nanocomposites were used for the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 that saturated NaOH solutions.The Hg-lamp was used as a source of UV lighting.The suspensions of CdS-MMT and the TiO 2 (Evonic P25) catalyst used for comparison were irradiated during 24 h.Fig. 6 shows the yields of reaction products depending on irradiation time.Using both catalysts, hydrogen was the main reduction product in the gas phase.The large amounts of methane and low amounts of carbon monoxide were determined as well.The observed yields of the reaction products (lmol g A1 of the catalyst) were found to be in the order: H 2 ) CH 4 CO.As a whole, using CdS-MMT, these gases were produced in 4-8-fold higher amounts than with TiO 2 .In addition, methane, hydrogen and carbon monoxide were produced continually during the whole experiments in the presence of CdS-MMT.In case of TiO 2 , the products were generated only after the first 8 h. Methane and carbon monoxide were likely formed by the reaction of CO 2 with hydrogen.The photoreduction mechanism was supposed as follows hm CO The amounts of nascent CO and CH 4 are given by kinetic constants of reactions ( 6) and ( 7), such as change of the standard Gibbs energy (DG) and the equilibrium constant (K).The reaction ( 7) is characterized by DG A130.8 kJmol and K 8.5 A 10 22 while the thermodynamic constant of reaction ( 6) are DG 20 kJmol and K 3.1 A 10 A4 (all constants at 25 C) 44. At pH 6.6 during the photoreactions, about 60 of CO 2 (aq) exists in the form of hydrogen carbonate There is some controversy about the reduction of HCO A 3 45.For example, Hori 46 found hydrogen carbonate to be an electroinactive compound that can be decomposed to CO 2 and then reduced according to Eqs. ( 6) and (7).The role of HCO A 3 during the photoreduction with CdS-MMT is unclear.Such high amounts of hydrogen and methane formed during the photoreduction with ZnS-MMT were also observed 35.Hydrogen generated according to Eq. ( 5) likely played the key role in the complex photoreduction mechanism.The different amounts of hydrogen produced in the presence of the photocatalysts can be explained in terms of conduction band energy (E CB ).It holds for CdS and TiO 2 : E CB (CdS) E CB (TiO 2 ) E (H H 2 ) 2.From this order, it follows that hydrogen ions generated from water were reduced to hydrogen more extensively when CdS-MMT was present.The band gap energy of TiO 2 used in this study was estimated at 3.08 eV 47.

title: Conclusions
content: CdS nanoparticles were precipitated in aqueous solutions of cadmium and sulphide ions.Cetyltrimethylammonim was added for their stabilization.The CdS band-gap energy was estimated at 2.63 0.09 eV by means of the Tauc plot.Using the band-gap energy, the nanoparticle mean size was calculated at 5 nm. CdS nanoparticles of cubic structure were deposited on the MMT surface.Their mean size about 5 nm determined from the TEM micrographs was very close to that calculated in micellar aqueous dispersions.CTA was intercalated in the MMT interlayer and also acted as a binder among CdS and MMT particles forming their agglomerates with size from 100 nm to 500 nm.MMT served as a carrier of CdS nanoparticles that were attached to its external surface. In spite of the low content of CdS (6 wt.), CdS-MMT exhibited significant photoactivity in the photoreduction of carbon dioxide.The reaction gaseous products, such as hydrogen, methane and carbon monoxide, were generated in 4-8-fold higher amounts than in the presence of the commercial TiO 2 photocatalyst.On the basis of the changes in standard Gibbs energies, the main reduction mechanism was supposed to be based on the formation of hydrogen that reduced CO 2 to CH 4 . Fig. 1.Absorption spectrum of CdS nanoparticles (A) and estimation of CdS band-gap energy by means of Tauc plot (B). Fig. 2. X-ray diffraction patterns of MMT and CdS-MMT. Fig. 3. SEM micrographs of CdS-MMT.View on CdS-MMT surface (A), SEM map of cadmium (B), SEM map of sulphur (C). Fig. 4. TEM micrographs of CdS-MMT. Fig. 5. Particle size distribution by volume.CdS-MMT (solid line), MMT (dashed line).The distribution of nanoparticels with size 100 nm are shown in the inset. Fig. 6.Yields of reaction products in dependence on irradiation time.CdS-MMT (A), TiO 2 (B). Selected characteristics of TiO 2 Evonic P25. P. Praus et al. Journal of Colloid and Interface Science 360 (2011) 574-579 This work was supported by the Czech Science Foundation (Project No. P107111918), the Regional Materials Science and Technology Centre (CZ.1.052.1.0001.0040), the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sport of the Czech Republic (Project No. MSM 6198910016) and by the EU Project No. CZ.1.052.1.0003.0069.The authors would like thank M. Heliova and Dr. M. Valaskova (both from the Nanotechnology centre, VSB-Technical University of Ostrava) for the SEM and XRD measurements, respectively.
